title: Hot and Sour Soup

seo.imageAlt: A steaming bowl of authentic Chinese Hot and Sour Soup, garnished with green onions and chili oil, ready to be enjoyed.
seo.imageCaption: Savor the rich, complex flavors of a perfectly balanced homemade Hot and Sour Soup.

description: Experience the vibrant, complex flavors of a classic Hot and Sour Soup, a staple of Chinese cuisine known for its tantalizing balance of spicy heat and tangy sourness. This comforting soup combines tender pork, silken tofu, and earthy mushrooms in a rich, savory broth, thickened to perfection. It is a hearty and deeply satisfying dish that warms the soul and awakens the palate.

instructions.title: Prepare Ingredients
instructions.text: Slice pork tenderloin thinly into matchsticks. Drain and cut tofu into similar matchsticks. Rehydrate dried shiitake and wood ear mushrooms in hot water for 20 minutes, then slice. Prepare cornstarch slurry by mixing cornstarch with 1/4 cup cold water until smooth. Beat the egg in a small bowl.

instructions.title: Simmer Broth and Solids
instructions.text: In a large pot, bring chicken broth to a boil. Add the sliced pork and cook until just opaque, about 2 minutes. Then add the rehydrated shiitake and wood ear mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and tofu. Bring the soup back to a gentle simmer.

instructions.title: Season the Soup
instructions.text: Stir in the rice vinegar, soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. Taste and adjust seasonings as needed; you may want more vinegar or white pepper for a stronger kick. Maintain a gentle simmer.

instructions.title: Thicken the Broth
instructions.text: Slowly pour the cornstarch slurry into the simmering soup in a thin stream, stirring constantly, until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. If it becomes too thick, add a little more broth or water.

instructions.title: Add Egg Ribbons
instructions.text: Once the soup has thickened, slowly drizzle the beaten egg into the simmering soup while stirring gently in one direction. This will create delicate egg ribbons throughout the soup. Cook for about 1 minute until the egg is set.

instructions.title: Serve Hot
instructions.text: Ladle the hot and sour soup into individual bowls. Garnish generously with chopped green onions and a drizzle of chili oil, if desired, for extra heat and flavor. Serve immediately.

tips: For authentic flavor, do not skimp on white pepper; it provides the unique 'hot' element distinct from chili spice. Adjust to your preference.
If you prefer a vegetarian version, omit the pork and use vegetable broth. Ensure your soy sauce is mushroom-based or a vegetarian variety.
Leftover Hot and Sour Soup can be stored in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days. Reheat gently on the stovetop, adding a splash of broth if it has thickened too much.
article:
Hot and Sour Soup: A Culinary Journey into Flavor

Few dishes capture the essence of Chinese cuisine quite like Hot and Sour Soup. It’s a symphony of flavors – a tantalizing dance between fiery heat and zesty tang, coupled with a comforting, savory depth. This beloved classic, found on menus from bustling street stalls to upscale restaurants, offers a uniquely satisfying experience that warms the soul and awakens the palate. More than just an appetizer, it’s a bowl of complex textures and invigorating tastes that leaves a lasting impression. If you've ever wanted to recreate that magic at home, you're in the right place, as we guide you through an authentic Hot and Sour Soup recipe, ensuring you can bring this restaurant favorite right to your kitchen.

## What is Hot and Sour Soup?

Hot and Sour Soup, or "Suan La Tang" in Mandarin, is a staple of Chinese culinary tradition, particularly prominent in Sichuan and Hunan provinces, though it has countless regional variations. Its defining characteristics are, as the name suggests, its spiciness ("hot") and its sourness ("sour"). The heat typically comes from white pepper, chili oil, or sometimes fresh chilies, providing a sharp, stimulating warmth. The sourness is primarily derived from rice vinegar, which adds a bright, acidic counterpoint to the richness of the broth.

Beyond these two dominant flavors, the soup is celebrated for its intriguing textural medley. Common ingredients include slivers of pork, firm tofu, rehydrated dried shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, and bamboo shoots, all julienned to a fine consistency. The broth itself is thickened with a cornstarch slurry, giving it a velvety, substantial mouthfeel, often finished with delicate ribbons of whisked egg that float gracefully throughout the soup. It's a dish that's both invigorating and deeply comforting, making it a perennial favorite across the globe.

## Ingredients You Need

Crafting the perfect Hot and Sour Soup recipe begins with assembling the right ingredients. Freshness and quality are key to achieving that authentic, balanced flavor. Here’s what you’ll need:

For the Broth Base:
*   6 cups chicken or vegetable broth (low sodium preferred)
*   3 tablespoons soy sauce
*   1 tablespoon dark soy sauce (for color and depth)
*   1 tablespoon sugar
*   1 teaspoon sesame oil
*   ½ teaspoon ground white pepper (or more, to taste)

For the "Hot" and "Sour" Elements:
*   ¼ cup rice vinegar (unseasoned)
*   1-2 tablespoons chili oil (optional, for extra heat)

Main Solids:
*   4 ounces lean pork, thinly sliced or julienned (pork loin or tenderloin works well)
*   8 ounces firm tofu, pressed and julienned
*   4-5 dried shiitake mushrooms, rehydrated, stems removed, and thinly sliced
*   ½ cup wood ear mushrooms, rehydrated and thinly sliced (or 1 small can, drained and sliced)
*   1 (8-ounce) can bamboo shoots, drained and julienned

Thickener and Garnish:
*   3 tablespoons cornstarch
*   ½ cup cold water (for cornstarch slurry)
*   2 large eggs, whisked
*   2 green onions, thinly sliced (for garnish)
*   Fresh cilantro, chopped (for garnish, optional)

Optional Aromatics:
*   1 teaspoon grated fresh ginger
*   2 cloves garlic, minced

## Step-by-Step Instructions

Mastering the Hot and Sour Soup recipe is surprisingly straightforward, requiring a bit of prep and careful layering of flavors. Follow these steps for a delicious bowl every time:

1.  **Prepare Ingredients:** If using dried mushrooms, rehydrate them in warm water for at least 30 minutes until soft. Squeeze out excess water, remove stems, and slice thinly. Slice pork, tofu, bamboo shoots, and wood ear mushrooms into thin, matchstick-sized pieces. In a small bowl, whisk together cornstarch and cold water to create a slurry. Whisk the eggs in another small bowl.
2.  **Sauté Aromatics (Optional):** In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat a teaspoon of cooking oil over medium heat. If using, add grated ginger and minced garlic, and sauté for about 30 seconds until fragrant.
3.  **Cook Pork:** Add the julienned pork to the pot and cook until lightly browned and no longer pink, about 2-3 minutes.
4.  **Build the Broth:** Pour in the chicken or vegetable broth. Add soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, wood ear mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and tofu. Bring the mixture to a gentle simmer.
5.  **Season and Flavor:** Once simmering, stir in the white pepper and rice vinegar. Taste the soup and adjust the "hot" (more white pepper or chili oil) and "sour" (more vinegar) elements to your preference. This step is crucial for balancing the signature flavors.
6.  **Thicken the Soup:** Give the cornstarch slurry a quick whisk again, then slowly pour it into the simmering soup in a thin stream, stirring constantly. Continue stirring until the soup thickens to your desired consistency. If it's too thick, add a little more broth or water.
7.  **Add Egg Ribbons:** With the soup still at a gentle simmer, slowly drizzle the whisked eggs into the soup in a thin stream while stirring gently in a circular motion. This will create beautiful, delicate egg ribbons. Stop stirring once the egg is cooked, usually within 30 seconds.
8.  **Finish and Serve:** Remove the pot from the heat. Stir in the sesame oil. Ladle the Hot and Sour Soup into bowls and garnish generously with sliced green onions and fresh cilantro, if desired. Serve immediately.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

Achieving that perfect balance in your homemade Hot and Sour Soup recipe involves a few key techniques and considerations:

*   **Taste and Adjust:** The "hot" and "sour" components are highly personal. Continuously taste the soup as you add white pepper and vinegar. Don't be afraid to add more of either until it hits your sweet spot. Some prefer a milder tang, others a fiery kick.
*   **Quality Ingredients Matter:** A good quality chicken or vegetable broth forms the foundation of your soup. Fresh shiitake mushrooms (if you can find them) or well-rehydrated dried ones will offer superior flavor.
*   **Don't Over-Thicken:** Add the cornstarch slurry gradually. You can always add more, but it’s harder to thin out an overly thick soup. Aim for a consistency that coats the back of a spoon without being gloopy.
*   **Perfect Egg Ribbons:** The trick to delicate egg ribbons is a slow, steady drizzle of whisked egg into a gently simmering (not boiling) soup while stirring continuously in one direction.
*   **Pork Prep:** Slicing the pork thinly against the grain will ensure it cooks quickly and remains tender. Marinating it briefly in a little soy sauce and cornstarch can also add flavor and tenderness.
*   **Spice Level Customization:** For extra heat, consider adding a pinch of dried chili flakes or a dash of your favorite hot sauce along with the chili oil.

## Serving Suggestions

Hot and Sour Soup is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in many ways. It’s a fantastic starter, whetting the appetite for the main course with its bold flavors.

*   **As an Appetizer:** This is its most common role in Chinese restaurants. Serve small bowls before a larger meal.
*   **Light Lunch or Dinner:** Paired with a simple bowl of steamed white rice or some fresh spring rolls, it makes for a satisfying and flavorful light meal on its own.
*   **Alongside Other Chinese Dishes:** It complements a wide array of Chinese cuisine, from fried rice and lo mein to stir-fries and dumplings. Its vibrant flavors cut through richer dishes, providing a refreshing contrast.
*   **Garnish for Extra Flair:** Don't skimp on the garnishes! Freshly sliced green onions and cilantro add a burst of freshness and color. A final drizzle of chili oil or a sprinkle of crispy fried shallots can elevate the experience even further.

## Variations and Substitutions

This Hot and Sour Soup recipe is wonderfully adaptable, allowing you to tailor it to your preferences or dietary needs.

*   **Vegetarian/Vegan:** Easily make this soup vegetarian or vegan by using vegetable broth and omitting the pork. Increase the amount of tofu, or add other vegetables like sliced carrots, water chestnuts, or more varieties of mushrooms. Ensure your soy sauce is vegan-friendly.
*   **Different Proteins:** Instead of pork, you can use thinly sliced chicken breast or shrimp. Add shrimp towards the end of cooking, as they cook very quickly.
*   **Mushroom Medley:** Experiment with different types of mushrooms such as oyster mushrooms, enoki mushrooms, or dried lily flowers for varied textures and earthy notes.
*   **Spice Level:** For a deeper, more complex heat, consider adding a few dried red chilies to the broth as it simmers, or a dash of Szechuan peppercorns for a unique numbing spice.
*   **Sourness Alternatives:** While rice vinegar is standard, a touch of black vinegar (Chinkiang vinegar) can add a richer, deeper, and slightly sweeter sour note, along with a darker hue to the soup.
*   **Thickness Preference:** If you prefer a thinner soup, simply use less cornstarch slurry. For a heartier soup, you can add more firm ingredients.

Armed with this comprehensive guide, you're now ready to tackle the ultimate Hot and Sour Soup recipe. Whether you're a seasoned cook or a beginner in the kitchen, this flavorful and comforting soup is a rewarding dish to master. Enjoy the incredible balance of heat, tang, and savory goodness that makes this Chinese classic an enduring favorite worldwide.
